edge: Equivariant networks capture the inductive bias about the symmetry of the learning task by building those symmetries into the model. In this paper, we study how equivariance relates to generalization error utilizing PAC Bayesian analysis for equivariant networks.
In general, the bound indicates that using larger group size in the model improves the generalization error substantiated by extensive numerical experiments.

By using perturbation analysis of equivariant networks in Fourier domain for each layer, we derive norm-based PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds. The bound characterizes the impact of group size, and multiplicity and degree of irreducible representations on the generalization error and thereby provide a guideline for selecting them.
By using perturbation analysis of equivariant networks in Fourier domain for each layer, we derive norm-based PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds.
A canonical example is a convolutional layer for which the translation of the input image (over R2) leads to translation of convolutional features.
Equivariant neural networks are built using representation theoretic tools to achieve general equivariance.
General equivariant networks are built using irreducible representations of a group, as seen in works by Shawe-Taylor and Wood, Cohen and Welling, and Weiler et al.
An open question is about how to design feature spaces and combine representations efficiently in neural networks.
Building a G equivariant model involves choosing a group G to consider, which is crucial for achieving equivariance.
Continuous symmetries in the data require approximating G-equivariance using a discrete subgroup H < G, as continuous symmetries cannot be built into the model directly.
